sentences.text: Yes, Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Yes, he was most of the time there.
And I know that he-- after what happened at the hospital, that she told him.
He knew about it, because he was very kind to me, and always with a smile.
And he-- he didn't-- he never said-- I never talked to him.
I was never him.
But sometimes if I came into the room, you know?
I know that she told him everything.
But she did so much for me.
She saved me.
If not for her, I wouldn't be here now today.
And in such circumstances, I don't think that another person would do it, because the doctor at the hospital, I don't know if he was a Nazi or not.
I don't know.
But I know, apart from saving me and keeping me in her home, she paid a lot for me.
And it came out later.
And when we were liberated in '45, first the Americans came there, the army.
And all-- they didn't feel anything for them.
They were free.
Now, every-- it was-- they danced on the streets there.
All those peasants, mostly peasants, were there brought in forced labor.
They just-- without even saying goodbye, they left, everything half work, half done, everything.
So it was-- all over Germany, wherever they were, these people, they-- they left them.
It was-- for them, it was a day of mourning for the Germans, because imagine a big farm like the one where I was left without-- she couldn't be everywhere.
She couldn't work.
Even she worked herself.
She helped out too.

But a house, two children, and parents-in-law, two elderly people, and suddenly no one helping in the house, and no one helping in the fields, and no one attending the cows or the other-- everywhere, wherever people worked.
Sorry.
And when, finally, I wanted to leave.
And I still have a very bad conscience that I did nothing about her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I didn't think much.
I thought only of going back and finding out if anyone was left alive from my family, and especially my child, if she's alive still, if she's there.
I couldn't think of anything else that she's not alive, although she was left in awful circumstances without food, and had children even not once beat her.
I've seen it.
And I couldn't even tell them, don't do it, because their mother would say, see?
My children are not your children.
So all this, I knew.
And I wanted to go back as quick as possible.
Now, she didn't-- so I went up, and wanted to say goodbye to her, not saying anything that I know that she saved me to tell her face to face.
I still couldn't do it.
And she still didn't tell me that I'm Jewish, never.
She was very nice.
So I went to say goodbye.
She didn't want to speak to me.
She locked herself in a room.
And I have seen it.
Before she locked herself, she tore her hair out of the head.
She started to scream.
She was like mad.
Screamed-- she was always very quiet, very nice, and behaved very nicely.
I have seen that she took full hands of hair, she tore her hair like this.
And she didn't say a word.
She didn't mention my name, nothing.
She just screamed and tore-- she was just like mad.
I can't imagine-- often, at night time, I think what happened to her?
Especially everybody knew that after the Americans, the Soviets will occupy that part of Germany, the Russians.
She thought that I know maybe the language.
They didn't know exactly, but European languages.
And she saved my life, now I have to stay.
And I think it was she-- I should have done it, if I wouldn't be what I am, leaving a family, leaving children, leaving a child, leaving sisters and brothers, not knowing what happened to them.
I should have stayed.
And she helped me to help her.
But I couldn't.
I couldn't, especially I know that my child I have to search-- to see, because the Germans left.
The Russians were there.
And I couldn't get into [INAUDIBLE].
So I had to search for her to find out where she is.
So I was torn [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to pieces.
What shall I do now?
And I still-- of course, that was stronger, because I had a child.
She never believed me.
She said, you never married.
She thought I'm a young girl.
You're not married.
You're lying, she said.
You don't have a child.
I don't believe you.
You just wanted to leave me till now.
I just-- I just have it on my-- heavily on my conscience.
Night by night, I don't sleep.
And I wake up, why didn't I earlier do it?
Maybe I don't know what happened, because we know that the Russians when they came, they took over everything.
They took over her-- they took away her farm from her.
I know everything.
I know it.
I know what happened in Poland when the Russians came, because they took away the-- the mill from my in-laws, my grandfather of my late husband.
They took away everything.
And they took away from her.
And from [NON-ENGLISH], he was a big, fat man, very good-- a good man, a jolly man.
But I'm sure that they killed him.
I'm quite sure.
I know what the Russians are.
We have had them.
So I lived through the Russians.
And I lived through the Germans.
And now I left her.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was halfway to [NON-ENGLISH], and I ran back.
And they said, wonder where you're going?
We won't wait for you.
You won't find your way.
We could still hear shootings in the forests around.
The Americans were there first at the beginning.
We could hear around.
And there were always soldiers.
But we walked through a main street to [NON-ENGLISH].
I had those documents that I showed you, the torn ones, the old ones, from [NON-ENGLISH].
So we went.
And I was halfway.
And I ran back.
And I said, wait for me.
I have to go back.
I didn't-- she didn't open the door.
And I wanted to say goodbye.
I just felt it, that I should do something about it.
I couldn't go away without seeing her, because I don't think that she lives after what happened there later with the Russians with the two children alone.
Not that I got used to her.
I knew all the time that I am different from them.
And I-- and I-- and I have to go back home.
But she saved my life.
I wouldn't be here today.
My daughter wouldn't have a mother if she wouldn't have saved me.
Nothing helped.
I knocked on the door, and said, go away, go away, go away, go away.
I don't want to know you.
And I ran back to the people.
And we matched a few kilometers to [NON-ENGLISH].
You have seen it on the map.
[NON-ENGLISH] was the first [INAUDIBLE].
The Americans were there.
And we stayed there quite a while, until there was not-- it was, I think, in '46 that-- it was from the Red Cross.
I think I have a photo of the Red Cross on my-- I have to find the photo I have there for the-- with the Red Cross here, Red Cross there.
They looked for nurses to work there during our stay there in [NON-ENGLISH].
But there are a lot of people, I think not hundreds but thousands.
And later, they took me as a transport, because they were-- the doctors were still in the-- still in the fields.
We could hear shootings around.
It wasn't straight away liberated all Germany.
The Americans, and the Soviets, it was going on, and going on, all the times the war, different places.
And-- but people wanted to go back-- not all, some wanted to stay there.
And from there, to migrate wherever they could, United States or England.
And so we had no doctors.
And through the Red Cross, it was established who wants to go back home.
And there were, I think, 8,000 that we had in [NON-ENGLISH].
They all wanted to go back to Germany-- not Germany, Poland, Czechoslovakia, Romania, Hungary, mostly that part, eastern part of Europe.
In other places, there were others.
I even didn't know that about three kilometers, I think, from the place where I stayed before, my sister was there as a non-Jewess, too.
She went before me, younger sister, who is now in France.
She survived too.
And she was not far from me.
We didn't-- each other didn't know about ourselves.
We didn't know nothing.
And I-- I didn't believe that she was alive.
I believed, rather, that my brother-- I loved him dearly, my brother, eldest brother.
And they [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] never found him.
Pardon?
No, dates, we never thought of dates there.
Who would-- if I would sometimes write a day, or write a letter, or think, we didn't live a normal life to remember.
I often didn't remember it was Monday or Sunday.
We didn't-- that was not a life that-- that-- in a normal life, you have to think of a date, of a month, a year.
You write a-- you write here, you write there, write a check-- we didn't need it.
We just lived from day to day.
I didn't know, often, if it's-- I got up in the morning, if it's night or morning.
When we walked, we walked to [INAUDIBLE], yes?
And where-- I think straight away, we left, as soon as we heard that the war was over, although it wasn't over there, not everywhere, but slowly, slowly.
In [NON-ENGLISH], they searched for [INAUDIBLE], as I said, and-- to register with them.
And because I wanted to go back, and there was not normal transport, no transport, no way to go back to Poland to search for relatives of survivors.
But I thought maybe if I joined the Red Cross, through them maybe I can go back.
There was no-- no train.
Nothing was there.
You couldn't go back.
People didn't travel just after the war, or during the war.
And when I came over, first I had to start working there, because I was still there, until they got together a big-- 8,000 people, a transport.
And I had to be the one who went with them, because there was no one else.
There was only an American captain of the-- it wasn't a normal train.
Again, they put together some wooden carriages for a train with a machine, you know.
Somewhere, they constructed a train for 8,000 people.
And there was only an American officer, captain.
He had to have someone going to all those different countries to get in contact with the officials.
And I was only one for 8,000 people.
That was the paper I have here when I arrived in Poland.
And on the way, I had-- people were ill.
First of all, they gave me food that I had to-- in those Red Cross packages, cartons of some food, [INAUDIBLE] what was there, I don't even remember exactly.
In the one carriage of that train, we kept all the food under the key.
I had the key.
Otherwise, the people would take it out, everything, under the key.
And once daily, I had to give rations to 8,000 people.
I couldn't find myself.
I had to have someone, a helper, there, two helpers.
But I was the only one, no one else.
They didn't have enough-- among the people were somewhere, someone else's.
And they had to look after the people.
On the way, I had a few corpses.
People died.
There was no doctor.
And I had just a limited supply of some-- some injections and some drugs, some tablets, something like this.
And some sick people on the way, I had to deliver to check-- we stopped at each country to deliver the people.
And there was only one American captain who never was with me.
He was in somewhere else.
He had all the documents, who he's-- he's caring what people, where they go, from where they came.
It was a chaos.
And he-- so-- we-- I delivered to Czechoslovakia, I remember Prague, in a hospital, about 20 sick people-- very ill people.
I don't know if they lived or not.
And then I had some in-- no, from Czechoslovakia, I came-- I think Transylvania on the way to Poland, up there from Czechoslovakia.
And then we came to Poland [NON-ENGLISH].
I never could-- never dreamt of [INAUDIBLE].
It was in the Russian hands to [NON-ENGLISH].
And from [NON-ENGLISH] to Krakow.
And when I was free, I could stay two weeks.
I had documents.
I could stay and go back.
I went about three times like this.
Only I didn't-- no one forced me.
But I wanted to find out.
Meanwhile that I stayed there for two weeks, I could stay, to find out about my child.
Oh, no, no, no that's a long story.
That's a story for itself.
But I want to tell you, in Krakow when I was, I went to the Red Cross.
And, of course, I told them what happened, that I finished with them.
And I had to go back to-- and again back for transport people.
They didn't pay me for it, only with food.
There, I went to the Red Cross.
I didn't go anymore.
I had to go more.
I didn't.
I tell you what happened.
It's a long story.
I went to the Red Cross.
And I Said, now that I'm free, I'm looking for a child.
Could you help me?
I helped you.
I brought up the people here.
I didn't have money.
I didn't have food.
In the transport, I had food, but otherwise not.
How can I find-- I had a child.
And I told them how she was left in Lwow, under what circumstances.
They said, all right.
Everything is all right.
We would help you if we could.
How do you want to find a little child in a stack of hay?
It's like a needle in a stack of hay, they said.
Just if you lose a needle in stack of hay, look for it.
Who can help you?
Can you go to Lwow?
I was still under the assumed name, still under Grabowienska, not Jewish.
And if you go to there, it is-- Lwow is in Soviet's hands.
You can't go there.
They'll take you.
Anybody who went there, they said is a spy.
They take you as a spy, they will imprison you.
So better don't go.
I didn't go.
You know what happened I found out later?
Not far from-- from-- I think from Krakow, not far, is a little place [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH].
I have to look up here.
I never did.
It said [NON-ENGLISH] was the first-- established the first Jewish [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] orphanage, the first Jewish orphanage.
She was there.
But how she went there, I didn't tell you the story.
And I was about 30 kilometers from her, didn't know that she is there.
And I left and went back to Poland-- to Germany.
And how I went back to Germany is a different story.
She was there.
And I didn't know.
Why she was there, that's a different story how I found her.
That's a different story.
But she was there.
And I didn't know.
And she didn't know that I-- I didn't know that she's alive at all.
And she didn't know that I am alive.
But she was at that orphanage.
How she-- later tell me how she came to that orphanage.
She was not at all in Poland.
She was by different people taken out from one place to another.
She didn't want to go.
She said, Jesus Mary, the Jews will kill me.
That how she was brought up, that Polish family.
And she was in the churches every day for hours.
Till now, she had on her knees, marks.
She had sores from kneeling on the cold, stony floor in the churches.
Till now, she showed me, Mom, that's from there.
A mark, and it was whipping, you know?
Never seen a doctor.
But fortunately enough, it's all right.
Nothing happened to the bones.
So I couldn't find her, although she was not far from me.
And I went back to the Red Cross.
And I begged that maybe you can find-- no, impossible.
I tell you what, they say.
We have here about 80 families, Jewish families, survivors, who want to cross the border.
That was a border of Czechoslovakia, Kudowa.
I remember all the places.
Kudowa, I don't think anyone else could remember that all.
It was named Kudowa.
And she said, Kudowa is a border Czechoslovakia and Poland.
And that-- you know, the Transylvania is under eastern Polish border, southeastern.
If you take those people through Kudowa, they want to immigrate to the United States.
But Poland doesn't let them out.
So illegally, you can take them out, if you can cross the Polish border.
You as one with the Red Cross here, Red Cross there, you can go.
Nothing will happen to you.
No one will touch you-- International Red Cross.
But those people, if you take them, you go out with them as the one who is leading them, maybe you'll get through from Poland out to where Czechoslovakia is, you know?
I said, all right.
I didn't think anything will happen to me.
I went, took the group of Jewish people.
No one knew that I'm Jewish.
No one knew.
They took me all for Red Cross.
They were afraid to talk to me, from Red Cross, Polish.
We went.
On the border Poland and Czechoslovakia in Kudowa, the police say, where are you going?
I said, it's through the Red Cross.
I have all documents here.
You have to-- two-- two police on the border-- we have to go over.
And these people are mostly sick people, survivors.
You have to let us through.
They think over, they talk together, they let us.
But we had to stay until to Kudowa.
And stay in Kudowa, it was a camp after the-- terrible, awful.
We slept on straw.
We could see lice.
It was after the army was there, the German army was there.
And we couldn't lay down of that, just straw, you know?
Without any coverings.
On this, we had to sleep there three weeks.
What happened?
I saved them, not knowing even.
What happened?
It was in winter, cold, ice, icy-- it was [INAUDIBLE], there, the ground.
And I walked outside once.
And it was so slippery, the snow was frozen ice.
And I slipped on the ground outside.
We were in-- in, like, barracks, you know?
I don't know you call-- camps, it was a work camps there for the soldiers, very dirty, terrible.
Yes, they gave me a little food to give out to the people, a few of those boxes food.
And I went out once in the afternoon, and fell on my back on the icy ground.
It hurt me terribly.
I could hardly walk.
And then I thought, I'll go.
No one was there outside.
It was in the afternoon.
And I thought that maybe I go further to the border of Czechoslovakia.
Maybe I'll find a doctor somewhere there, because I knew what I have to do.
Only to warm it up, they tell me, I don't know in Polish, [NON-ENGLISH].
So I have to find a doctor, even if I cross to Czechoslovakia-- it was Czechoslovakia, but on the border.
We didn't cross it right.
I started to walk, and came, and you've seen written, doctor.
I don't know what he was, Dr.
So-and-so.
I crossed over the border.
No one was there.
I came into him.
And he had one look at me, and said, are you Jewish?
I said, yes.
I am Jewish too, a Jewish doctor.
That's why he asked.
It was after the war.
I wasn't afraid to admit.
I said, yes.
A doctor, what would he do with me?
And it was after-- it was in Czechoslovakia, not in Germany.
So he said, I am Jewish too.
And he helped me straight away, free.
He knew that I have no money.
And I told him the story.
And he was on the board of-- Jewish board-- Jewish community service, whatever.
Straight away, my presence, he asked me to come a few days.
For that, it was heating, and helped me.
And in my presence, he rang to someone to the-- one of the top of the Jewish community service.
And they came in.
A whole group came in, because we were only Jewish people [INAUDIBLE], and helped us.
Police let us through.
So we left that place.
But it was awful.
We stayed I think nearly a month there.
Four weeks we stayed, until I fell.
And that helped.
Otherwise, I wouldn't go for help.
I didn't know anyone in Czechoslovakia.
But that helped.
So we got through.
And we came-- from Poland, it was back in Germany, yes, but another part, not where I was before back in Germany.
And the whole group went to Bamberg, I remember.
Not Nuremberg, that's before Nuremberg, a different place, the nearest from Czechoslovakia.
And we went to Bamberg.
In Bamberg, there's already established Jewish [NON-ENGLISH], how is [NON-ENGLISH]?
Like a community center.
And I met there quite a number of people, mostly were survivors, you know, concentration camp survivors most.
And all the time that Pole went after me, he was actually Ukrainian.
But he saved me too, not knowing that I'm Jewish.
There people told him, I told them-- told him.
And then he left.
I don't know-- have no idea where he left or what.
From Bamberg, we were sent groups wherever one wanted to go.
And I was sent-- the community service was in Meiningen.
And I wrote it down there.
And I was sent to [NON-ENGLISH].
I told-- they asked for if anyone has a profession, where to work, what to do for the time being until we migrate somewhere for good.
So they sent me to Nuremberg.
That was actually [NON-ENGLISH] Nuremberg.
[NON-ENGLISH] was before Nuremberg.
It was a half a city, [NON-ENGLISH] Nuremberg.
So when I started to work in [NON-ENGLISH], I was still under the non-Jewish name.
And then I felt, something is wrong.
After the war, and I still have to-- also many people kept the non-Jewish name even here.
In Australia, I found a lot of people that they stayed as they are.
I felt no.
It's an insult to my family, I thought.
If I want to get away from them, I go back to my name.
I'm Mehr.
I'm not-- and I was-- Wanda was an assumed name.
But because no one knew me as otherwise, I had no one left.
So I put two, Ida Wanda.
I was Ida Ida, first name.
And I started to work.
Then I thought, no, I have to go back to my name.
I went to-- but we didn't have anything in [NON-ENGLISH].
I had to go to [NON-ENGLISH], to Meiningen.
There was a-- a-- like community center.
I went there.
And I came in there.
And they were mostly Hungarian Jews.
And I came in.
And I said [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I want to go to the head of the community.
They showed me someone there, came up.
And I said, but, once at home-- as I said, my mother used to talk to the children Polish, because young children Poland-- in Lwow, mostly attending non-Jewish-- we didn't have Jewish schools there.
It was only one gymnasium on [NON-ENGLISH], where you had to pay a lot of money.
Not everybody had could pay for the children attending that gymnasium.
And they learned a little bit Hebrew there too.
And because I was in a state gymnasium, not in the Hebrew one, and not in Lwow, it was in Grodek, I didn't know very well Yiddish.
